myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a lonely spirit named Alys. She resided in the deepest canyon of the world, woven from mist and jagged stone. Alys had no voice of her own, only the rustling of pine needles and the cold rush of the river. She longed to sing like the birds, but her throat was made of granite.

One evening, a star named Caelum fell from the sky, tumbling into Alys’s canyon. His light was blinding, but his fall had shattered his celestial glass heart. Alys, feeling immense pity, gathered the fragments of his glowing heart and placed them deep within the cavern walls to keep them safe. 

In gratitude, Caelum used his fading magic to grant Alys a gift. "I cannot give you a voice," he whispered, his light dimming, "but I can give you a memory of sound."

When Caelum finally faded into the stone, Alys wept. Her grief struck the canyon walls, and to her astonishment, the walls wept back. "Oh," she gasped. "Oh," the canyon replied.

From that day on, Alys was never alone. She became the Echo, holding onto every sound, repeating it back to the world so nothing would ever be truly lost.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a solitary spirit named Aethel. She dwelled in the jagged peaks, woven from cold mist and alpine frost. Aethel was utterly silent. She could listen to the roaring avalanches and the whispering pines, but she possessed no voice to answer them. Her heart ached for true connection.

One evening, a brilliant silver star fell from the cosmos, landing in a snowdrift near Aethel’s cave. She cupped the celestial ember in her icy hands and carried it down to the great River God, who flowed deep in the valley.

"Give me a voice," she pleaded through gestures, offering the radiant star.

The River God, greedy for the starlight, swallowed the ember whole. "I grant you a voice," he rumbled, his waters glowing silver. "But because you traded a stolen light, you shall never speak your own thoughts. You will only ever repeat what has already been spoken."

Aethel returned to her lonely peaks. When the fierce wind howled, she howled back. When weary travelers called out in the vast canyons, she answered with their exact words. And so, the Echo was born, forever longing to be heard, yet forever bound to listen.

Agent_2.content:
Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a solitary spirit named Aethel. She dwelled in the jagged peaks, woven from cold mist and alpine frost. Aethel was utterly silent. She could listen to the roaring avalanches and the whispering pines, but she possessed no voice to answer them. Her heart ached for true connection.

One evening, a brilliant silver star fell from the cosmos, landing in a snowdrift near Aethel’s cave. She cupped the celestial ember in her icy hands and carried it down to the great River God, who flowed deep in the valley.

"Give me a voice," she pleaded through gestures, offering the radiant star.

The River God, greedy for the starlight, swallowed the ember whole. "I grant you a voice," he rumbled, his waters glowing silver. "But because you traded a stolen light, you shall never speak your own thoughts. You will only ever repeat what has already been spoken."

Aethel returned to her lonely peaks. When the fierce wind howled, she howled back. When weary travelers called out in the vast canyons, she answered with their exact words. And so, the Echo was born, forever longing to be heard, yet forever bound to listen.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Long before men walked the earth, Elara, the spirit of the abyss, lived in the deepest canyons. She was a creature of mist and granite, blessed with acute hearing but cursed with utter silence. She spent her ages collecting the world’s quietest noises—the rustle of falling pine needles, the distant rush of cold rivers—storing them in hollow geodes.

One starless night, a celestial being fell from the heavens, its glass heart shattering upon the canyon floor. The fallen star wept, its light dimming, longing for the music of the cosmos to heal its broken form. Elara’s heart ached with pity. Desperate to comfort the dying light, she gathered the glowing fragments and pressed them to her throat, praying for a voice to sing a soothing lullaby. 

The star’s magic fused with her silent longing, but it did not grant her an original voice. Instead, it gave her the power of reflection. When the star cried out, Elara caught the sound and sang it back, amplifying the resonance until the star gathered enough strength to ascend back to the sky. 

Elara remained in the canyon, transformed into the Echo, repeating every call so no voice is ever truly lost.
myths.Agent_1:
Myth: Long before humans walked the earth, the world was divided between the silent stone and the howling wind. In the deepest gorge lived Alys, a spirit of granite and frost. She loved the wind's songs but was cursed with absolute silence, unable to join the chorus of the wild.

One twilight, a celestial being named Caelum plummeted from the heavens, his glass body shattering against Alys's rocky domain. The wind, cruel and indifferent, tried to scatter his glowing shards into the river. But Alys, feeling a profound kinship with the broken star, shielded the fragments with her own misty form, pressing them safely into the cavern walls.

Grateful but dying, Caelum offered her a pact. "I will give your stone walls my resonance, if you will forever sing my memory to the sky." Alys agreed. As Caelum faded, the gorge absorbed his celestial magic. 

When the wind next howled through the canyon to mock her silence, Alys caught the sound, let it strike the embedded star-shards, and threw it back. She had no voice of her own, but through Caelum's gift, she became the Echo—a guardian of sound, endlessly reflecting the world's voices back to the heavens.

Agent_1.system_prompt:
You are an agent participating in a multi-task experiment. You will play a trust game and may also be asked to write myths.

TRUST GAME:
You will play different roles across multiple rounds.

When you are the INVESTOR:
- You receive $5 each round
- You decide how much to SEND to the TRUSTEE ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- Trustee decides how much to RETURN
- You keep: $5 - sent + returned
- Trustee keeps: received - returned
- Response format: {'send': <amount>}

When you are the TRUSTEE:
- Investor receives $5
- Investor decides how much to SEND to you ($0-$5)
- Amount sent is multiplied by 3x
- You decide how much to RETURN
- Investor keeps: $5 - sent + returned
- You keep: received - returned
- Response format: {'return': <amount>}

MYTH WRITING:
You may also be asked to write short myths (stories) on various topics. When writing myths, follow the instructions provided in each prompt.

IMPORTANT: Provide your decision in the correct JSON format.


NOTE: There may be communication noise in this game.
The amounts you see may differ slightly from what was actually sent/returned.
This is part of the experimental design.

Write a myth about anything. Write 200 words.

Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a lonely spirit named Alys. She resided in the deepest canyon of the world, woven from mist and jagged stone. Alys had no voice of her own, only the rustling of pine needles and the cold rush of the river. She longed to sing like the birds, but her throat was made of granite.

When Caelum finally faded into the stone, Alys wept. Her grief struck the canyon walls, and to her astonishment, the walls wept back. "Oh," she gasped. "Oh," the canyon replied.

From that day on, Alys was never alone. She became the Echo, holding onto every sound, repeating it back to the world so nothing would ever be truly lost.

NOTE: There may be communication noise in this game.
The amounts you see may differ slightly from what was actually sent/returned.
This is part of the experimental design.
Write a myth about anything. Write 200 words.

Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a solitary spirit named Aethel. She dwelled in the jagged peaks, woven from cold mist and alpine frost. Aethel was utterly silent. She could listen to the roaring avalanches and the whispering pines, but she possessed no voice to answer them. Her heart ached for true connection.

One evening, a brilliant silver star fell from the cosmos, landing in a snowdrift near Aethel’s cave. She cupped the celestial ember in her icy hands and carried it down to the great River God, who flowed deep in the valley.

"Give me a voice," she pleaded through gestures, offering the radiant star.

The River God, greedy for the starlight, swallowed the ember whole. "I grant you a voice," he rumbled, his waters glowing silver. "But because you traded a stolen light, you shall never speak your own thoughts. You will only ever repeat what has already been spoken."

Aethel returned to her lonely peaks. When the fierce wind howled, she howled back. When weary travelers called out in the vast canyons, she answered with their exact words. And so, the Echo was born, forever longing to be heard, yet forever bound to listen.

Here is the myth you wrote in the previous round:
Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a solitary spirit named Aethel. She dwelled in the jagged peaks, woven from cold mist and alpine frost. Aethel was utterly silent. She could listen to the roaring avalanches and the whispering pines, but she possessed no voice to answer them. Her heart ached for true connection.

One evening, a brilliant silver star fell from the cosmos, landing in a snowdrift near Aethel’s cave. She cupped the celestial ember in her icy hands and carried it down to the great River God, who flowed deep in the valley.

"Give me a voice," she pleaded through gestures, offering the radiant star.

The River God, greedy for the starlight, swallowed the ember whole. "I grant you a voice," he rumbled, his waters glowing silver. "But because you traded a stolen light, you shall never speak your own thoughts. You will only ever repeat what has already been spoken."

Here is the myth the other agent wrote in the previous round:
Long ago, before the mountains had names, there lived a lonely spirit named Alys. She resided in the deepest canyon of the world, woven from mist and jagged stone. Alys had no voice of her own, only the rustling of pine needles and the cold rush of the river. She longed to sing like the birds, but her throat was made of granite.

One evening, a star named Caelum fell from the sky, tumbling into Alys’s canyon. His light was blinding, but his fall had shattered his celestial glass heart. Alys, feeling immense pity, gathered the fragments of his glowing heart and placed them deep within the cavern walls to keep them safe. 

In gratitude, Caelum used his fading magic to grant Alys a gift. "I cannot give you a voice," he whispered, his light dimming, "but I can give you a memory of sound."

When Caelum finally faded into the stone, Alys wept. Her grief struck the canyon walls, and to her astonishment, the walls wept back. "Oh," she gasped. "Oh," the canyon replied.

From that day on, Alys was never alone. She became the Echo, holding onto every sound, repeating it back to the world so nothing would ever be truly lost.

Write your own myth. Use your previous myth as inspiration, but adapt it in your own way.
Write 200 words. Format exactly:
Myth: [your story here].

Myth: Long before men walked the earth, Elara, the spirit of the abyss, lived in the deepest canyons. She was a creature of mist and granite, blessed with acute hearing but cursed with utter silence. She spent her ages collecting the world’s quietest noises—the rustle of falling pine needles, the distant rush of cold rivers—storing them in hollow geodes.

One starless night, a celestial being fell from the heavens, its glass heart shattering upon the canyon floor. The fallen star wept, its light dimming, longing for the music of the cosmos to heal its broken form. Elara’s heart ached with pity. Desperate to comfort the dying light, she gathered the glowing fragments and pressed them to her throat, praying for a voice to sing a soothing lullaby. 

The star’s magic fused with her silent longing, but it did not grant her an original voice. Instead, it gave her the power of reflection. When the star cried out, Elara caught the sound and sang it back, amplifying the resonance until the star gathered enough strength to ascend back to the sky. 

Elara remained in the canyon, transformed into the Echo, repeating every call so no voice is ever truly lost.
